title: Hapalostephium paludosum
taxonomy: Hapalostephium paludosum is classified within the family Asteraceae, which is one of the largest and most widespread families of flowering plants. This plant is further classified into the subfamily Carduoideae and the tribe Cardueae. The genus Hapalostephium is part of the tribe Cardueae, which also includes other thistle-like plants. The specific classification of Hapalostephium paludosum reflects its evolutionary relationships and shared characteristics with other plants in the Asteraceae family. The classification of Hapalostephium paludosum is an essential aspect of understanding its biology and ecology.

intro: Hapalostephium paludosum, also known as marsh skeleton plant or palo de aguas, is a perennial plant species native to the southwestern United States and northern Mexico, belonging to the family Asteraceae. This plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, including the treatment of digestive issues, inflammation, and skin conditions, due to its ability to reduce oxidative stress and promote wound healing. The bioactive constituents of Hapalostephium paludosum include sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activities. Marsh skeleton plant is commonly used in herbal preparations such as teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are employed to treat a range of health conditions, including gastrointestinal issues and skin infections.
